entry.title: The Thought Process Behind: Manifold Optimization on the Magnitude Torus for Fourier Phase Retrieval

link.title: The Thought Process Behind: Manifold Optimization on the Magnitude Torus for Fourier Phase Retrieval

entry.content:
The work “Manifold Optimization on the Magnitude Torus for Fourier Phase Retrieval” is accepted to ICIP 2026. This blog post is a record of how we discovered this topic, what our initial motivation is, and what approaches we have taken that led to the finalized conference version.

Motivation

Working under Prof. Homer H. Chen on optical systems, it is quite a far stretch for us to suddenly stumble upon the problem of Fourier phase retrieval. This part goes over exactly the backstory.

Furthermore, we also give a brief description of our motivation to analyze the Fourier phase retrieval problem from a geometric point of view.

Optical interpolation

Originally, we were considering the following problem: how can we use a simple optical system to enhance the resolution of a display? No matter how high th resolution a digital image is, the perceived resolution or clarity of the image is inherently limited by the resolution of the physical display. The importance of such problem need not to be further emphasized.

An approach that we came up with was to utilize a “4\(f\)-system” to physically realize the interpolation operation.


See the figure above. The input field \(U\) at \(0f\) comes from the left of the system, and after passing through a multiplicative modulation \(X\) at \(2f\), the output field \(Z\) exits from the right at \(4f\). By the principles of Fourier optics, a \(2f\) lens system (from the front focal plane of the convex lens to its rear focal plane) can be seen as performing a Fourier transform.

The image above shows the resulting output field \(Z\) if the input field \(U\) is coherent. For incoherent inputs, the output field becomes

\[\left|Z(\boldsymbol{x})\right|^2 = \left|U(\boldsymbol{x})\right|^2 * \left|\mathscr{F}\{X\}(\boldsymbol{x})\right|^2,\]

where \(*\) is the convolution operation. Therefore, the task of designing a suitable \(X\) that creates high-resolution output images boils down to the classical Fourier phase retrieval problem.

To reiterate, the (discrete) Fourier phase retrieval problem is that given amplitude measurements \(\boldsymbol{y} \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge0}^{n\times1}\), the \(d\)-dimensional \(n\times n\) discrete Fourier transform matrix \(A\), and a signal domain constraint set \(\mathcal{B} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n\times1}\), find a signal \(\mathbb{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times1}\) that satisfies

\[\begin{align*} &\left|A \boldsymbol{x}\right| = \boldsymbol{y}, \\ &\text{s.t. } \boldsymbol{x} \in \mathcal{B}. \end{align*}\]

For our study, we primarily worked with the nonnegative signal constraint \(\mathcal{B} = \mathbb{R}_{\ge0}^{n\times1}\). The details can be seen from our work (Perng et al., 2026).

Projected gradient descent

Many algorithms were proposed to tackle the phase retrieval problem, and they have extremely excellent performances! However, what bugs me is that I do not fully understand why these algorithms work so well beyond local heuristic explanations, and in fact, neither do most people.1

My instinct when solving this problem is not the Gerchberg—Saxton (GS) algorithm or the hybrid-input-output algorithm, but instead the gradient descent algorithm. I firmly believe that geometry is the way to understand algorithms, and what better way to understand gradient descent via plotting its optimization landscape.

First, we need to introduce a loss function for we optimize over. Here we used the magnitude-based loss function

\[\ell(\boldsymbol{x}) := \left\|\, |A\boldsymbol{x}| - \boldsymbol{y}\right\|_2^2.\]

By performing projected gradient descent over the loss function \(\ell\), the iterations will converge to local minima. It should be noted that the GS algorithm is equivalent to the projected gradient descent with stepsize \(\mu = 0.5\).

Sometimes, the algorithm is successful, and we can losslessly retrieve the signal (up to its trivial associates), but sometimes, the iterates stalls at weird places.

Take a look at the example above. Although the iteration eventually converges, there is this huge plateau that the iterates got stuck on. From this, it immediately occurred to me that the loss landscape of \(\ell\) and its intersection with \(\mathcal{B}\) must be really complicated.

At low dimensions, e.g., \(n = 3\), I was able to plot the loss landscape for the true one-dimensional signal \(\boldsymbol{x} = [0,1,2]\).


The black points marked are the trivial associates to \(\boldsymbol{x}\). And would you look at that! There exist nontrivial associates and they form a ring (a one-dimensional torus). I thought to myself, “I must know more about its geometry.”

The Magnitude Torus

The magnitude torus associated with the magnitude measurement \(\boldsymbol{y}\) is defined as

\[\mathbb{A}_{\boldsymbol{y}} = \left\{ \mathbb{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times1} \middle\vert \,\left|A \boldsymbol{x}\right| = \boldsymbol{y}\right\}.\]

It didn’t occur to us that this set is in fact a torus. In hindsight, this is quite trivial as we can easily assign

\[\boldsymbol{x} = A^{-1}(\boldsymbol{y} \odot \exp(\mathrm{i}\boldsymbol{\phi})),\]

while ensuring that the resulting \(\boldsymbol{x}\) is real. The remaining task is to actually characterize what symmetries the phases \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\) should satisfy before we can understand the dimensionality of the magnitude torus. Our exploration, however, took a completely different route.

Local dynamical point of view

From the one-dimensional signal with \(n=3\) we have seen earlier, it is believed that the solutions to \(|A\boldsymbol{x}| = \boldsymbol{y}\) is connected, maybe there is a way to traverse on such a set! This idea is later shown to be incorrect as we see from the paper that the magnitude torus consists of multiple disjoint tori. Nevertheless, let us see where this idea leads us to.

If multiple zeros to \(\ell\) are connected, then the shape of the landscape should locally be like a valley. I.e.,

\[\dim\left( \mathrm{ker}\left( \nabla^2\ell(\boldsymbol{x})\right)\right) > 0\;\;\;\;\;(\ell(\boldsymbol{x}) = 0),\]

where \(\nabla^2\ell(\boldsymbol{x})\) is the Hessian. Note that the directions in the kernel of the Hessian are the directions that leads from a zero of \(\ell\) to another.

The Hessian of \(\ell\) at an arbitrary point is derived to be

\[\begin{aligned} \nabla^2\ell(\boldsymbol{x}) &= \mathbf{Re}\left\{A^{\mathsf{H}} \mathrm{diag}\left(2\cdot\boldsymbol{1} - \boldsymbol{y} \odot |A \boldsymbol{x}|^{-1}\right) A \right\} \\ &\hspace{0.5cm}+ \mathbf{Re}\left\{A^{\mathsf{H}} \mathrm{diag}\left(\mathrm{sgn}(A \boldsymbol{x})^{2} \odot \boldsymbol{y} \odot |A \boldsymbol{x}|^{-1}\right) A^{*}\right\}. \end{aligned}\]

Note that \(\boldsymbol{1}\) is the vector of ones. Its evaluation at a zero \(\boldsymbol{x}^\star\) can be readily calculated to be

\[\boxed{ \nabla^2\ell(\boldsymbol{x}^\star) = I + \underbrace{\mathrm{Re}\left\{A^{-1} \mathrm{diag}\left(\mathrm{sgn}(A \boldsymbol{x}^\star)^{2}\right) A^{*}\right\}}_{M(\boldsymbol{x}^\star)} }.\]

Positive-semidefiniteness of the Hessian

From a simple MATLAB calculation, we see that the spectrum of \(\nabla^2\ell\) at zeros are really simple: almost half of the eigenvalues are 0’s, and the other half are 2’s. To be more precise, for two-dimensional signals of size \(n = M \times N\), we have the following classification

\((M,N)\) # of 0’s # of 2’s
(even,even) \(\frac{MN-4}{2}\) \(\frac{MN+4}{2}\)
(even,odd) \(\frac{MN-2}{2}\) \(\frac{MN+2}{2}\)
(odd,odd) \(\frac{MN-1}{2}\) \(\frac{MN+1}{2}\)

Can we show that these observations are true for all possibilities? Immediately from the boxed equation above, we can see that the loss function is positive-semidefinite at the global minima: for any real vector \(\boldsymbol{v}\), the second term can be bounded by

\[\begin{align*} \boldsymbol{h}^\mathsf{T} \mathbf{Re}\left\{A^{\mathsf{H}} \mathrm{diag}\left(\mathrm{sgn}(A \boldsymbol{x}^\star)^{2}\right) A^{*}\right\} \boldsymbol{h} &= \boldsymbol{h}^\mathsf{T} \mathbf{Re}\left\{F^{\mathsf{T}} \mathrm{diag}\left(\mathrm{sgn}(A^* \boldsymbol{x}^\star)^{2}\right) A\right\} \boldsymbol{h} \\ &= \mathbf{Re}\left\{(A \boldsymbol{h})^{\mathsf{T}} \mathrm{diag}\left(\mathrm{sgn}(A^* \boldsymbol{x}^\star)^{2}\right) (A \boldsymbol{h})\right\} \\ &\ge -\boldsymbol{h}^\mathsf{T} \boldsymbol{h} \end{align*}\]

Henceforth, the Hessian $\nabla^2\ell(\boldsymbol{x}^\star)$ is positive-semidefinite. The equality holds if and only if $\mathrm{sgn}(A\boldsymbol{h})^2 \odot \mathrm{sgn}(A^*\boldsymbol{x}^\star)^2 = -\boldsymbol{1}$.

Tangent vectors

Next, to find out the spectrum exactly, we must find the eigenvectors. By a miraculous observation, we tested out calculating \(\nabla^2 \ell(\boldsymbol{x}^\star) [\boldsymbol{x}^\star] = 2 \boldsymbol{x}^\star\)! Interestingly enough, the global minima \(\boldsymbol{x}^\star\) is itself an eigenvector of eigenvalue 2! This inspires us to calculate \(\nabla^2 \ell(\boldsymbol{x}^\star) [P\boldsymbol{x}^\star]\), where \(P\) is a translation of the image \(\boldsymbol{x}^\star\).

Since \(P\) is a translation, its action on \(\boldsymbol{x}^\star\) is equivalent to a phase shift in the Fourier domain. Therefore, we can introduce a phase \(\boldsymbol{\phi}_P\) that satisfies

\[A(P\boldsymbol{x}^\star) = (A\boldsymbol{x}^\star) \odot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \boldsymbol{\phi}_P}.\]

Continue on with the calculations:

\[\begin{aligned} M(\boldsymbol{x}^\star) \cdot P\boldsymbol{x}^\star &= \mathbf{Re}\left\{A^{-1} \mathrm{diag}\left(\mathrm{sgn}(A \boldsymbol{x})^{2}\right) A^{*}P\boldsymbol{x}\right\} \\ &= \mathbf{Re}\left\{A^{-1} \mathrm{diag}\left(\mathrm{sgn}(A \boldsymbol{x})^{2}\right) \left((A^*\boldsymbol{x}) \odot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i}\boldsymbol{\phi}_P}\right)\right\} \\ &= \mathbf{Re}\left\{A^{-1} \left((A\boldsymbol{x}) \odot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i}\boldsymbol{\phi}_P}\right)\right\} \\ &= \mathbf{Re}\left\{A^{-1} AP^{-1} \boldsymbol{x}\right\} \\ &= P^{-1} \boldsymbol{x}. \end{aligned}\]

The second to last equality is by the fact that $\boldsymbol{\phi}_{P^{-1}} = -\boldsymbol{\phi}_P$. We can thus construct the eigenvectors to be

\[\begin{align*} \nabla^2\ell(\boldsymbol{x}^\star) \left[P \boldsymbol{x}^\star + P^{-1} \boldsymbol{x}^\star\right] &= 2 \left(P \boldsymbol{x}^\star + P^{-1} \boldsymbol{x}^\star\right), \\ \nabla^2\ell(\boldsymbol{x}^\star) \left[P \boldsymbol{x}^\star - P^{-1} \boldsymbol{x}^\star\right] &= \boldsymbol{0} && (P\boldsymbol{x}^\star \neq P^{-1}\boldsymbol{x}^\star). \end{align*}\]

The vectors \((P-P^{-1}) \boldsymbol{x}^\star\) are the tangent vectors to the magnitude torus as described by Barnett et al.2 Given them, we are able to traverse all path-connected zeros of \(\ell\) to \(\boldsymbol{x}^\star\).

To be precise, the curve

\[\exp\left((P - P^{-1})t\right) \boldsymbol{x}^\star\]

lies on the magnitude torus for all translation \(P\) and all \(t\).

A new basis

The matrix

\[\exp\left((P - P^{-1})t\right)\]

is, however, non-periodic. In fact, we can find out that it has multiple components all oscillating in a different frequency by performing spectral decomposition. What are the eigenvectors of \(P\)? They are, coincidentally, the Fourier vectors \(\boldsymbol{a}_{k}\)! That is how we finally obtained the basis vectors

\[\{\mathbf{Re}\{\boldsymbol{a}_k\}, \mathbf{Im}\{\boldsymbol{v}_k\}\}_{k \in \mathcal{N}_{p} \cup \mathcal{N}_{c}} = \{\boldsymbol{u}_k, \boldsymbol{v}_k\}_{k \in \mathcal{N}_{p} \cup \mathcal{N}_{c}}\]

seen in our work.

Dimensions of the magnitude torus

The dimension of the magnitude torus is equal to number of unique eigenvectors \((P - P^{-1}) \boldsymbol{x}^\star\).

Global

From the local dynamical point of view, we see that the magnitude torus is indeed a surface traced by mutually orthogonal circles, i.e., it is a torus.

The global point of view, on the other hand, treats the magnitude torus as the intersection of mutually orthogonal hyperplanes and hypercylinders. This view gives us an explicit count of the number of tori and their dimensions.

Traversing the torus

Having obtained the basis \(\{\boldsymbol{u}_k, \boldsymbol{v}_k\}_{k \in \mathcal{N}_{p} \cup \mathcal{N}_{c}}\), we can move on the magnitude torus via

\[\begin{aligned} &\exp\left((\boldsymbol{u}_k \boldsymbol{v}_k^\mathsf{T} - \boldsymbol{v}_k \boldsymbol{u}_k^\mathsf{T})t\right) \boldsymbol{x}^\star, &k \in \mathcal{N}_{c}. \end{aligned}\]

The actual computation can be made simpler by using the Rodrigue’s formula (or Euler’s identity for \(\mathfrak{so}(3)\)) or by separating the action on each coordinates in the torus parameterization. The latter is way faster in implementations.

A fun thing demonstrating the torus parameterization is to interpolate between two points on the magnitude torus. Below we show one such example where we linearly interpolate between the horizontally shifted trivial associates. The figure on the right shows the torus angles \(t_k\) for \(k\in\mathcal{N}_{c}\).

Manifold Optimization

In hindsight, the two algorithms we have developed are quite obvious—it is natural that such algorithms can lead the iterations closer to the local optima. Yet, coming up with them took us on quite a convoluted thought process.

TorusGD

The idea of TorusGD is simple, perform gradient descent in the torus coordinates, this idea is an immediate consequence of the parameterization we obtained that allows us to traverse on the torus.

The problem lies in how we can numerically implement it.

The first task is to obtain the basis \(\{\boldsymbol{u}_k, \boldsymbol{v}_k\}_{k \in \mathcal{N}_{p} \cup \mathcal{N}_{c}}\), i.e., how do we obtain the row indices \(\mathcal{N}_{p}\) and \(\mathcal{N}_{c}\) that define unique Fourier constraints on the set of possible signals?

The MATLAB code we used was created by ChatGPT. Its logic is simple, a vector \(\boldsymbol{a}_k\) is created by taking the Kronecker product of its one-dimensional Fourier vector components, each of length equal to the corresponding size \(N_k\) (\(k \in [D]\)): Using the notation in our paper, we have \(n = N_1 \cdot \ldots \cdot N_D\), and let

\[\boldsymbol{f}^{(\ell)}_{k} = [1,\omega_k,\omega_k^2,\ldots,\omega_k^{N_\ell-1}]^\mathsf{T}, \omega_{k} = \exp\left(\mathrm{i}\frac{2\pi}{N_\ell}k\right),\]

then the corresponding Fourier vector is

\[\boldsymbol{a}_k = \bigotimes_{\ell=1}^{d} \boldsymbol{f}^{(\ell)}_{k_\ell}\]

Next, let us discuss how the indices \(\mathcal{N}_p\) and \(\mathcal{N}_{c}\) are determined. Suppose \(D=2\), \(N_1=M\), and \(N_2 = M\). We denote the Fourier vector by \(\boldsymbol{a}_{(k,\ell)} = \boldsymbol{f}^{(1)}_{k} \otimes \boldsymbol{f}^{(2)}_{\ell}\). In the code, phi stands for \(\boldsymbol{a}\), u{k+1} stands for \(\boldsymbol{f}^{(1)}_{k}\), and v{l+1} stands for \(\boldsymbol{f}^{(2)}_{\ell}\).

for k = 0:M-1
    for l = 0:N-1

        k2 = mod(-k, M);
        l2 = mod(-l, N);

        phi = u{k+1}.' * v{l+1};

        % -------- plane (self-conjugate) -------- 
        if k==k2 && l==l2
            idx_p = idx_p + 1;
            vp{idx_p} = reshape(real(phi), [M * N, 1]);

        % -------- cylinder (conjugate pairs) --------
        elseif (k < k2) || (k==k2 && l < l2)
            idx_c = idx_c + 1;

            vc{idx_c,1} = reshape(real(phi), [M * N, 1]);
            vc{idx_c,2} = reshape(imag(phi), [M * N, 1]);
        end
    end
end

See the code excerpt above. An index \((k,\ell)\) is in \(\mathcal{N}_{p}\) if both \(\boldsymbol{f}^{(1)}_k\) and \(\boldsymbol{f}^{(2)}_\ell\) are real. This is determined by the following condition.

if k==k2 && l==l2

Check that the above condition holds for \(M\) and \(N\) being either even or odd.

On the other hand, an index \((k,\ell) \in \mathcal{N}_{c}\) comes in conjugate pair. Therefore, we only pick one in the pair. This is implemented by the condition

elseif (k < k2) || (k==k2 && l < l2)

This condition states that we keep \((k,\ell)\) if \(\boldsymbol{f}^{(1)}_k\) is not real and \(k < M/2\), or if \(\boldsymbol{f}^{(1)}_k\) is real but \(\boldsymbol{f}^{(2)}_\ell\) is not and \(\ell < N/2\). This concludes the explanation to our code.

dualPGD

The need for coming up with another algorithm other than being satisfied with TorusGD is that TorusGD runs really slow. Not only slow, but the precalculations and storage of the \(\{\boldsymbol{u}_k,\boldsymbol{v}_k\}\) are resource demanding. In fact, we cannot even run image signals larger than \(41\times41\) on our laptops.

The algorithm we finally came up with predates the name dualPGD. It is only after the algorithm was formulated did we realize that it was in fact dual to the PGD algorithm.

Its motivation is from our exploration of the gradient flow limit for TorusGD, where \(\mu \rightarrow 0\): Let \(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}}\) be the gradient flow and \(s\) be the time parameter, i.e., \(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}} = \boldsymbol{x}(\boldsymbol{t}(s))\). The gradient flow over \(\mathbb{T}^d\) is

\[\frac{\mathrm{d} \boldsymbol{t}}{\mathrm{d} s} := - \nabla(F\circ \boldsymbol{x})(\boldsymbol{t}(s)).\]

The induced flow over the magnitude torus is

\[\frac{\mathrm{d} \widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}}}{\mathrm{d} s} = \boldsymbol{x}_{*,\boldsymbol{t}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} \boldsymbol{t}}{\mathrm{d} s}\right),\]

where \(\boldsymbol{x}_{*,\boldsymbol{t}}\) is the pushforward of torus parameterization, i.e.,

\[\boldsymbol{x}_{*,\boldsymbol{t}}:T_{\mathbb{t}}\mathbb{T}^d \rightarrow T_{\boldsymbol{x}(\mathbb{t})}\mathbb{A}_{\boldsymbol{y}}.\]

It is at this moment that it suddenly occurred to me that what if I simply replace the pushforward map by the projection onto the tangent space of \(\mathbb{A}_{\boldsymbol{y}}\)? By having \(\boldsymbol{x}_{k+1} = P_{\mathcal{A}}(\boldsymbol{x}_{k} + \boldsymbol{v})\), we can simply let \(\boldsymbol{v}\) be a suitable descent direction to obtain another phase retrieval algorithm.

Having this idea in mind, it was only a few calculations away to make sure that \(P_{\mathcal{A}}\) is a retraction and that the resulting algorithm is the dual to the PGD.

NOTE

When we were proving that \(P_{\mathcal{A}}\) is a retraction, it is required from the steps that we have

\[N_{\boldsymbol{x}} = \mathrm{ker}\left(\mathbf{Im}\left\{\mathrm{diag}(A\boldsymbol{x}) A^*\right\}\right).\]

This is how the explicit characterization of the normal space was discovered.

Comparing the two algorithms

Below is an illustration of the two algorithms.


The two algorithms operate under different metric. If we let the stepsize \(\mu \rightarrow 0\), then the two gradient descent algorithms becomes gradient flow. We can compare them in this regime.

For TorusGD, we have its gradient flow being

\[\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{d} \widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}}}{\mathrm{d} s} &= \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} s} \left(\sum_{k\in\mathcal{N}_{p}} s_k \boldsymbol{u}_k + \sum_{k\in\mathcal{N}_{c}} 2 y_k \left(\cos t_k \boldsymbol{u}_k + \sin t_k \boldsymbol{v}_k\right)\right) \\ &= \sum_{k\in\mathcal{N}_{c}} 2 y_k \left(-\sin t_k \boldsymbol{u}_k + \cos t_k \boldsymbol{v}_k\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} t_k}{\mathrm{d} s} \\ &= \underbrace{\mathrm{row}\left\{\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{x}}{\partial t_k}\right\}_{k \in \mathcal{N}_{c}}}_{S} \frac{\mathrm{d} \boldsymbol{t}}{\mathrm{d} s} \\ &= S\left(-\nabla (F\circ \boldsymbol{x})\right) \\ &= - S \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial \boldsymbol{x}} \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{x}}{\partial \boldsymbol{t}}\right)^\mathsf{T} \\ &= - SS^\mathsf{T} \nabla F, \end{aligned}\]

where \(\nabla F := (\partial F/\partial\boldsymbol{x})^\mathsf{T}\). On the other hand, for dualPGD,

\[\frac{\mathrm{d} \widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}}}{\mathrm{d} s} = - \mathrm{proj}_{T_{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} \mathbb{A}_{\boldsymbol{y}}}\left(\nabla F(\boldsymbol{x})\right).\]

To understand their relationship, let us expand \(SS^\mathsf{T}\):

\[\begin{aligned} SS^\mathsf{T} &= \mathrm{row}\left\{\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{x}}{\partial t_k}\right\}_{k \in \mathcal{N}_{c}} \mathrm{col}\left\{\left(\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{x}}{\partial t_k}\right)^\mathsf{T}\right\}_{k \in \mathcal{N}_{c}} \\ &= \sum_{k \in \mathcal{N}_{c}} 2y_k^2 \boldsymbol{\tau}_k \boldsymbol{\tau}_k^\mathsf{T} \end{aligned}\]

where

\[\boldsymbol{\tau}_k = \sqrt{2} \left(-\sin t_k \boldsymbol{u}_k + \cos t_k \boldsymbol{v}_k\right)\]

is the \(k\)th unit tangent vector at \(\boldsymbol{x}\). On the other hand,

\[\begin{aligned} \mathrm{proj}_{T_{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}}}} &= \sum_{k \in \mathcal{N}_c} \boldsymbol{\tau}_k \boldsymbol{\tau}_k^\mathsf{T}. \end{aligned}\]

From these calculations above, it is easy to see that the two flows are different. Yet, their difference is simply a scaling by \(2y_k^2\) along the \(\boldsymbol{\tau}_k\) direction for \(k \in \mathcal{N}_{c}\).

Stationarity Condition

When we talk about the stationarity condition, we meant the conditions that \(\boldsymbol{x}\) should hold if and only if either TorusGD or dualPGD halts under small stepsizes.

The requirement that stepsizes should be small reflects that our domain, the magnitude torus, is not convex. Though small stepsizes may have you stay at a stationary point, large stepsizes may take you to its antipodal point on the torus.


Look at the figure above, which shows the change of position after an iteration of dualPGD with varying stepsizes \(\mu\). Note that the point \(\boldsymbol{x}\) here is NOT a global minima, but instead a local minima.

On the other hand, the figure below shows the case where \(\boldsymbol{x}\) here IS near a global minima. The two behaviors are obviously different. How to utilize this observation, however, is a task for another day.


For both the pictures above, we are considering the positive signal constraint. The result is quite universal for different test signals that satisfies the uniqueness condition3. We suspect this behavior has something to do with the contact geometry between \(\mathbb{A}_{\boldsymbol{y}}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) at the point, and the jump is due to the jumping to antipodal points.

Some more interesting thing to consider: The stationarity condition for dualPGD is

\[\begin{aligned} &\mathbf{Im}\left\{(A\boldsymbol{x})^* \odot (A \nabla F)\right\} = \boldsymbol{0} \\ &\mathbf{Im}\left\{(A\boldsymbol{x})^* \odot (A (\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta\nabla F))\right\} = \boldsymbol{0} \\ &\Leftrightarrow \forall k\in[n] \begin{dcases} \mathrm{sgn}(\boldsymbol{a}_k^\mathsf{H} \boldsymbol{x}) = \mathrm{sgn}(\boldsymbol{a}_k^\mathsf{H} (\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta\nabla F)), \\ \mathrm{sgn}(\boldsymbol{a}_k^\mathsf{H} \boldsymbol{x}) = \mathrm{sgn}(\boldsymbol{a}_k^\mathsf{H} (\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta\nabla F)) + \pi, \\ \boldsymbol{a}_k^\mathsf{H} (\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta\nabla F) = 0 \end{dcases} \end{aligned}\]

for all \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\). If it is case 1 for all \(k \in [n]\), then the latter statement is equivalent to

\[\begin{aligned} &\Leftrightarrow \mathrm{sgn}(A \boldsymbol{x}) = \mathrm{sgn}(A (\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta\nabla F)) \\ &\Leftrightarrow \boldsymbol{y} \odot \mathrm{sgn}(A \boldsymbol{x}) = \boldsymbol{y} \odot \mathrm{sgn}(A (\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta\nabla F)) \\ &\Leftrightarrow A^{-1} \left(\boldsymbol{y} \odot \mathrm{sgn}(A \boldsymbol{x})\right) = A^{-1} \left(\boldsymbol{y} \odot \mathrm{sgn}(A (\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta\nabla F))\right) \\ &\Leftrightarrow \underbrace{P_{\mathcal{A}}(\boldsymbol{x})}_{\stackrel{!}{=}\boldsymbol{x}} = P_{\mathcal{A}}(\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta\nabla F). \end{aligned}\]

For \(\alpha = 1\) and \(\beta = -\mu\), we recover the stationarity condition of TorusGD.

NOTE

What about the other two cases? And what about other values of \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\)?

We do not currently have a satisfactory answer for the two questions above. However, as far as simulated experiments and our geometric intuitions have shown, we can at least conclude that the stationarity condition for TorusGD and dualPGD coincide.

  1. See (1) “Geometry of the phase retrieval problem : graveyard of algorithms” by A.Barnett, C. L. Epstein, L. Greengard, and J. Magland and (2) “Phase retrieval with only a nonnegativity constraint” by Y. Tian and J. R. Fienup. ↩

  2. “Geometry of the phase retrieval problem : graveyard of algorithms” by A.Barnett, C. L. Epstein, L. Greengard, and J. Magland. ↩

  3. Theorem 5.1 in “Geometry of the phase retrieval problem,” by A.Barnett, C. L. Epstein, L. Greengard, and J. Magland, 2020. ↩
entry.summary: The motivation and thought process behind our work. Including experiments explored along the way.

entry.title: Inner Product of Vectors and Blades

link.title: Inner Product of Vectors and Blades

entry.content:
This post provides a proof to the identity of the inner product between a vector and an \(r\)-grade blade.

This post provides a proof to the important identity

\[\boxed{ \boldsymbol{v} \cdot (\boldsymbol{a}_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \boldsymbol{a}_r) = \sum_{i=1}^{r} (-1)^{i-1} (\boldsymbol{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{a}_i) (\boldsymbol{a}_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \breve{\boldsymbol{a}}_i \wedge \cdots \wedge \boldsymbol{a}_r) }.\]

I came up this proof by myself, though other authors have given a cleaner proof using grade projections. Before we go over the proof, let us see some application of this identity.

Examples

For vectors \(\boldsymbol{a}\), \(\boldsymbol{b}\), \(\boldsymbol{c}\), and \(\boldsymbol{d}\), we immediately have

\[\begin{align*} \boldsymbol{a} \cdot (\boldsymbol{b} \wedge \boldsymbol{c}) &= (\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{b}) \boldsymbol{c} - (\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{c}) \boldsymbol{b}, \\ \boldsymbol{a} \cdot (\boldsymbol{b} \wedge \boldsymbol{c} \wedge \boldsymbol{d}) &= (\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{b}) (\boldsymbol{c} \wedge \boldsymbol{d}) - (\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{c}) (\boldsymbol{b} \wedge \boldsymbol{d}) + (\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{d}) (\boldsymbol{b} \wedge \boldsymbol{c}). \end{align*}\]

The first one is actually just the familiar vector triple product in disguise:

\[\boldsymbol{a} \times (\boldsymbol{b} \times \boldsymbol{c}) = (\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{b}) \boldsymbol{c} - (\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{c}) \boldsymbol{b}.\]

This can be easily shown by noting that we have

\[\boldsymbol{a} \times \boldsymbol{b} := -(\boldsymbol{a} \wedge \boldsymbol{b})I\]

and the duality relation

\[(\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{b}) I = \boldsymbol{a} \wedge (\boldsymbol{b}I),\]

where \(I\) is the pseudoscalar in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), satisfying \(I^2 = -1\).

The Proof

First, we consider an orthonormal basis \(\{\boldsymbol{e}_i\}\) satisfying

\[V := \mathrm{span}\{\boldsymbol{a}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{r} = \mathrm{span}\{\boldsymbol{e}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{r}.\]

Let \(\boldsymbol{v} = \boldsymbol{v}_{\perp} + \sum_{i=1}^{r} v_{i} \boldsymbol{e}_{i}\), where \(\boldsymbol{v}_{\perp} \perp V\). Let the \(r\)-blade be \(A_{r} := \boldsymbol{e}_{1} \wedge \cdots \wedge \boldsymbol{e}_{r} = \boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}\). Then,

\[\begin{align*} \boldsymbol{v} \cdot A_{r} &= \left\langle \boldsymbol{v} A_{r} \right\rangle_{r-1} \\ &= \left\langle \boldsymbol{v}_{\perp} A_{r} + \sum_{i=1}^{r} v_{i} \boldsymbol{e}_{i} A_r \right\rangle_{r-1}. \end{align*}\]

The first term vanishes since it is of grade \(r+1\). Thus,

\[\begin{align*} \boldsymbol{v} \cdot (\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{r} v_{i} \boldsymbol{e}_{i} (\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}) \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{r} (\boldsymbol{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{e}_i) (-1)^{i-1} (\boldsymbol{e}_{1}\cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{i} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}). \end{align*}\]

Next, let us turn to the RHS of the target equation. We can expand the blade term as

\[\begin{align*} &\boldsymbol{a}_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \breve{\boldsymbol{a}}_i \wedge \cdots \wedge \boldsymbol{a}_r \\ &= \left(\sum_{j_{1} = 1}^{r} a_{1j_{1}} \boldsymbol{e}_{j_1}\right) \wedge \cdots \wedge \breve{\left(\sum_{j_{i} = 1}^{r} a_{ij_{i}} \boldsymbol{e}_{j_i}\right)} \wedge \cdots \left(\sum_{j_{r} = 1}^{r} a_{rj_{r}} \boldsymbol{e}_{j_r}\right) \\ &= \sum_{j_1,\ldots,j_r} (a_{1j_1} \cdots \breve{a}_{ij_i} \cdots a_{rj_r})(\boldsymbol{e}_{j_1} \cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{j_i} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{j_r}) \\ &= \sum_{j_1,\ldots,j_r} (a_{1j_1} \cdots \breve{a}_{ij_i} \cdots a_{rj_r}) \epsilon^{j_1\ldots j_r}_{1\ldots r}(\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{j_i} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}) \\ &= \sum_{j_i = 1}^{r} (-1)^{j_i-1} \det(M_{ij_i}) (\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{j_i} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}), \end{align*}\]

where \(\epsilon_{1\ldots r}^{j_1\ldots j_r}\) is the Levi-Civita symbol and \(M_{ij}\) is the \((i,j)\)-minor of the matrix \(a_{ij}\). Plug this back into the RHS of our target equation,

\[\begin{align*} \mathrm{RHS} &= \sum_{i=1}^{r} (-1)^{i-1} (\boldsymbol{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{a}_i) \sum_{j = 1}^{r} (-1)^{j-1} \det(M_{ij}) (\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{j} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}) \\ &= \sum_{i,j=1}^{r} (-1)^{i-1} \left(\sum_{k=1}^{r} a_{ik} \boldsymbol{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{e}_k\right) (-1)^{j-1} \det(M_{ij}) (\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{j} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}) \\ &= \sum_{j,k=1}^{r} \left(\sum_{k=1}^{r}(-1)^{i-1} a_{ik} \det(M_{ij}) \right) (-1)^{j-1} (\boldsymbol{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{e}_k) (\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{j} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}) \\ &= \sum_{j,k=1}^{r} (-1)^{j-1} \left(\det(a_{ij}) \delta_{kj}\right) (\boldsymbol{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{e}_k) (\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{j} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}) \\ &= \det(a_{ij}) \sum_{j=1}^{r} (-1)^{j-1} (\boldsymbol{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{e}_j) (\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \cdots \breve{\boldsymbol{e}}_{j} \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}). \end{align*}\]

By the relation we have earlier proven, we finally obtain the identity:

\[\begin{align*} \mathrm{RHS} &= \det(a_{ij}) \boldsymbol{v} \cdot (\boldsymbol{e}_1 \cdots \boldsymbol{e}_{r}) \\ &= \boldsymbol{v} \cdot (\boldsymbol{a}_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \boldsymbol{a}_r) = \mathrm{LHS}. \;\;\;\;\; \blacksquare \end{align*}\]

Conclusions

Although this theorem seems obvious if you interpret it as finding the component of the blade along the direction of \(\boldsymbol{v}\), while keeping the anti-commutativity of the wedge product in mind, it is not at all trivial.

In the notes to the previous geometric algebra study group I have held, we have actually never proven this identity, but instead treated it as true. Finally, after almost three years, I came back and filled in the gaps.
entry.summary: This post provides a proof to the identity of the inner product between a vector and an r-grade blade.

entry.title: Diffusion Stochastic Learning over Multi-Team Network Games

link.title: Diffusion Stochastic Learning over Multi-Team Network Games

entry.content:
The work “Diffusion Stochastic Learning over Multi-Team Network Games” is accepted to ICASSP 2026, with paper number 10037. One can find the details to the poster session in the link above: the poster session is SPTM-P2: Distributed Optimization I, at Tue., May 5th, 14:00 to 16:00 at Poster Area 2.

The author Wen Perng completed this work as a visiting student from National Taiwan University at the Adaptive Systems Laboratory at EPFL, Switzerland.

Supplementary Materials

The MATLAB code for the figures used in the paper can be found here, the proofs to the lemmas and theorems can be downloaded here (last updated: 2026/04/19).

Poster

The PDF file for the poster used in the conference can be found here.

Original Notes

The notes taken and the thought process behind this work is collected in my progress report at ASL, EPFL. (Last updated: Feb, 2026)
entry.summary: More information on the work "Diffusion Stochastic Learning over Multi-Team Network Games" done during my E3 internship and submitted to ICASSP 2026.

entry.title: Linear Recurrence Relations and the Spectrum of Toeplitz-Like Matrices

link.title: Linear Recurrence Relations and the Spectrum of Toeplitz-Like Matrices

entry.content:
This post discusses how the solution to linear recurrence relations relates to the spectrum of certain Toeplitz-like matrices. This observation connects to solving for the normal mode of spring-mass systems

Linear Recurrence Relations

A linear (homogeneous) recurrence relation of order \(k\) is of the form

\[\boxed{ x_n = a_{1} x_{n-1} + a_2 x_{n-2} + \cdots + a_{k} x_{n-k} }\]

can be efficiently rewritten as a matrix equation

\[\left[\begin{matrix} \textcolor{cyan}{x_{n}} \\ x_{n-1} \\ x_{n-2} \\ \ldots \\ x_{n-k+1} \end{matrix}\right] = \left[\begin{array}{cccc:c} \textcolor{cyan}{a_1} & \textcolor{cyan}{a_2} & \textcolor{cyan}{\cdots} & \textcolor{cyan}{a_{k-1}} & \textcolor{cyan}{a_k} \\ \hdashline 1 \\ & 1 \\ && \ddots \\ &&& 1 & 0 \end{array}\right] \left[\begin{matrix} x_{n-1} \\ x_{n-2} \\ x_{n-3} \\ \ldots \\ x_{n-k} \end{matrix}\right].\]

Writing the equation above as \(\mathbf{x}_n = A \mathbf{x}_{n-1}\), we can solve for any \(x_n\) given the initial condition \(\mathbf{x}_0\) by finding \(A^n\) for arbitrary \(n\).

TIP

From the theory of linear algebra, for a diagonalizable matrix \(A\), one can easily see that the elements of \(A^n\) are linear combinations of \(\lambda^n\)’s, where the \(\lambda\)’s are the eigenvalues of \(A\).

For non-diagonalizable matrices, on the other hand, its eigenvalues have geometric multiplicity larger than one. Suppose \(\lambda\) has a geometric multiplicity of \(3\), then still, the resulting matrix \(A^n\) is a linear combination of \(\lambda^n\), \(n\lambda^n\), and \(n^2\lambda^n\).

From the tip above, we see that the elements of \(\mathbf{x}_n\) are linear combinations of \(\lambda^n\), and so is \(x_n\) (here we assume the diagonalizability of \(A\), but the same idea applies for non-diagonalizable ones). This is the method of characteristic equation, useful for solving linear recurrence relations.

Spectrum of Toeplitz-Like Matrices

A Toeplitz matrix is a matrix with diagonals identified, i.e.,

\[\begin{matrix} A = \left[\begin{matrix} a_0 & a_1 & \cdots & \cdots & a_k \\ a_{-1} & a_0 & a_1 & & \vdots \\ \vdots & a_{-1} & a_0 & \ddots & \vdots \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \ddots & a_{1} \\ a_{-k} & \cdots & \cdots & a_{-1} & a_0 \\ \end{matrix}\right] \end{matrix}.\]

Although finding the exact spectrum to an arbitrary Toeplitz matrix is a daunting task, we will be analyzing a few specific matrices and obtain their exact spectrum. Furthermore, out of the four examples given, the last two are not exactly Toeplitz. In fact, I termed them Toeplitz-like as they are almost Toeplitz with sparse perturbations.

My exploration of these certain Toeplitz-like matrices stemmed from the analysis of the normal mode frequency of a spring-mass system. Given a system of differential equations describing the motion of such spring-mass system, one can turn it into a recurrence relation by plugging in an Ansatz. The Ansatz is parameterized by the normal mode frequency. Then, the difference equations can be rewritten into a matrix equation of the form \((A - \lambda I)x=0\) with the existence of non-trivial solutions relying on the \(\lambda\)’s being the eigenvalues to \(A\). The \(\lambda\)’s are related to the normal mode frequencies.

Spring-mass ring


The first example we consider is an \(N\) particle spring-mass chain forming a ring. This example naturally gives rise to a Toeplitz matrix that is also circulant. The equation of motion for the \(n\)th particle is

\[m \ddot{x}_n = -k(x_{n} - x_{n-1}) - k(x_{n} - x_{n+1}).\]

Plugging in the normal mode Ansatz \(x _n = a _n \cos(\omega t)\) and setting \(\lambda = \omega^2 m / k\), we have

\[\left[\begin{matrix} 2-\lambda & -1 & 0 & \cdots & 0& -1 \\ -1 & 2-\lambda & -1 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 2-\lambda & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 2-\lambda & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & -1 & 2-\lambda \end{matrix}\right] \left[\begin{matrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \\ \vdots \\ a_{N-1} \\ a_N \end{matrix}\right]\]

We can see that in this scenario, it is required that the \(\lambda\)’s be the eigenvalues of the matrix \(A\) defined as

\[A = \left[\begin{matrix} 2 & -1 & 0 & \cdots & 0& -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & -1 & 2 \end{matrix}\right]_{N\times N}.\]

To find the spectrum of \(A\), there will be two methods: one utilizes the circulant structure, and the other utilizes our knowledge of linear recurrence relations while incorporating the implicit boundary condition \(a_0 = a_{N}\). We will explore the latter one later.

We first notice that

\[A = 2 I_N - J_N - J_N^\mathsf{T}\]

where \(J_N\) is the permutation matrix

\[J_N = \left[\begin{matrix} 0 & 1 & & & 0 \\ & 0 & 1 \\ & & \ddots & \ddots \\ & & & \ddots & 1 \\ 1 & & & & 0 \end{matrix}\right].\]

Since \(J_N^{-1} = J_N^\mathsf{T}\), if the former has the eigenvalue-eigenvector pair \((\mu,v)\), then the latter has the eigenvalue-eigenvector pair \((1/\mu,v)\). Altogether, the eigenvalue of \(A\) will be \(\lambda = 2 - \mu - \mu^{-1}\). It can be easily found by induction that the characteristic polynomial to \(J_N\) is \(\mu^N - 1\). Thus, we have the eigenvalues to \(A\) being

\[\begin{aligned} \lambda_k &= 2 - \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}\frac{2\pi}{N}k} - \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i}\frac{2\pi}{N}k} &&(\mu_k = \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}\frac{2\pi}{N}k}, k = 1\sim N) \\ &= 2 - 2\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{N}k\right) &&(k = 1\sim N) \\ &= 4 \sin^2\left(\frac{\pi}{N}k\right) &&(k = 1\sim N). \end{aligned}\]

Spring-mass chain between two walls


The second scenario has both ends to a spring-mass chain fixed to walls. The particles satisfy the same equation of motion. However, the boundary conditions are now modified to

\[x_0(t) = x_{N+1}(t) = 0.\]

This means that we have the matrix \(A\) as

\[A = \left[\begin{matrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ & -1 & 2 \\ & & & \ddots & -1 \\ & & & -1 & 2 \end{matrix}\right]_{N\times N}.\]

This is again a Toeplitz matrix.

Unlike the previous case where the eigenvectors to the three matrix components are the same, the calculation for this case is much more intricate.

Instead of using the techniques from matrix algebra, let us instead refer back to the recurrence relations to find a solution. Using the same definition for \(\lambda\), the recurrence relation \(a_n\) satisfies is

\[(2-\lambda) a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n+1}\]

with boundary condition

\[a_0 = a_{N+1} = 0.\]

Using the characteristic method for solving linear recurrence relations, we plug in the Ansatz of \(a_n = \alpha_1 r_1^n + \alpha_2 r_2^n\) with \(r_1\), \(r_2\) satisfying

\[r^2 - (2-\lambda) r + 1 = 0.\]

There two solutions \(r _1\) and \(r _2\) satisfy \(r _1 + r _2 = 2 - \lambda\) and \(r _1 r _2 = 1\). For simplicity, denote \(r_1 = r\) and \(r_2 = r^{-1}\)

Next, we plug in the initial conditions:

\[\begin{align*} &\because a_0 = 0 = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2, \\ &\therefore \alpha_1 = \alpha = -\alpha_2, \\ &\because a_{N+1} = 0 = \alpha \left(r^{N+1} - r^{-(N+1)}\right), \\ &\therefore r^{2N+2} = 1, \\ &\Rightarrow r = \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \frac{\pi}{N+1}k} \;\;\;\;\;(k = 1\sim 2N+2). \end{align*}\]

However, note that \(r\) and \(r^{-1}\) may coincide, we can thus reduce the range of \(k\) to \(k=1\sim N+1\). Further, if \(k=N+1\), \(r = r^{-1}\), we will obtain \(a_n = 0\) which is the trivial solution and is discarded. Hence, \(k=1\sim N\). Lastly, we have

\[\begin{align*} \lambda &= 2 - (r_1 + r_2) \\ &= 2 - 2\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{N+1} k\right) \;\;\;\;\; (k = 1\sim N) \\ &= 4\sin^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2(N+1)} k\right) \;\;\;\;\; (k = 1\sim N). \end{align*}\]

These are the \(N\) eigenvalues to \(A\).

NOTE

Look how the tables have turned! In deriving the characteristic method for solving linear recurrence relations, we reformulate recurrence relations into a matrix equation and solve for the eigenvalues of the matrix.

Now, however, in order to find the spectrum to a Toeplitz matrix, we instead convert it into a recurrence relation with specific boundary conditions.

Spring-mass chain with one end fixed and another end free


Suppose the first mass is the one with spring connected to the wall and the \(N\)th mass is the one on the free end. For this case, the matrix \(A\) is

\[A = \left[\begin{matrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ & -1 & \ddots \\ & & & 2 & -1 \\ & & & -1 & 1 \end{matrix}\right]_{N\times N}.\]

and the boundary conditions are

\[a_0 = 0, a_{N+1} = a_{N}.\]

Note that \(A\) is no longer a Toeplitz matrix. However, it is still Toeplitz-like and it is tridiagonal. This allows us to convert it into a linear recurrence relation.

Similarly, using the characteristic method for linear homogeneous recurrence relations, the general solution will be \(a _n = \alpha _1 r^n + \alpha _2 r^{-n}\). We can plug in the boundary conditions to obtain

\[\begin{align*} &\because a_0 = 0, \\ &\therefore a_n = \alpha \left(r^n - r^{-n}\right), \\ &\because a_{N+1} = \alpha \left(r^{N+1} - r^{-N-1}\right) = a_{N} = \alpha \left(r^N - r^{-N}\right), \\ &\therefore r^{N+1} - r^N = - (r^{-N} - r^{-N-1}), \\ &\Rightarrow r^{2N+1} = -1 \;\;\;\;\;(r\neq0), \\ &\Rightarrow r = \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \frac{\pi}{2N+1}(2k-1)} \;\;\;\;\;(k = 1\sim N). \end{align*}\]

There should be \(2N+1\) solutions for \(r\). By the same argument as the last example, however, we can reduce it down to \(N\). The eigenvalues \(\lambda\) of \(A\) are finally obtained as

\[\begin{align*} \lambda &= 2 - 2\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2N+1} (2k-1)\right) &&(k = 1\sim N) \\ &= 4\sin^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2(N+1)} \left(k-\frac{1}{2}\right)\right) &&(k = 1\sim N). \end{align*}\]

Spring-mass chain with two ends free


This last example is also Toeplitz-like and tridiagonal. For this case, the matrix \(A\) is

\[A = \left[\begin{matrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ & -1 & \ddots \\ & & & 2 & -1 \\ & & & -1 & 1 \end{matrix}\right]_{N\times N}.\]

and the boundary conditions are

\[a_0 = a_1, a_{N+1} = a_{N}.\]

Similarly using the characteristic method for linear homogeneous recurrence relations, the general solution will be \(a _n = \alpha _1 r ^n + \alpha _2 r ^{-n}\). We can plug in the boundary conditions to obtain

\[\begin{align*} &\because a_0 = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = \alpha_1 r + \alpha_2 r^{-1} = a_1, \\ &\therefore \alpha_1 r = -\alpha_2, \\ &\because a_{N+1} = \alpha_1 r^{N+1} + \alpha_2 r^{-N-1} = \alpha_1 r^{N} + \alpha_2 r^{-N} = a_N, \\ &\therefore r^{N+1} - r^{-N} = r^N - r^{-N+1}, \\ &\Rightarrow r^{N+1} - r^N = r^{-N} - r^{-N+1}, \\ &\Rightarrow r^{2N} = -1, \\ &\Rightarrow r = \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \frac{\pi}{N} k} \;\;\;\;\;(k=1\sim N). \end{align*}\]

The eigenvalues \(\lambda\) of \(A\) are

\[\begin{align*} \lambda &= 2 - 2\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{N} k\right) \;\;\;\;\; (k = 1\sim N) \\ &= 4\sin^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2N} k\right) \;\;\;\;\; (k = 1\sim N). \end{align*}\]
entry.summary: This post discusses how the solution to linear recurrence relations relates to the spectrum of certain Toeplitz-like matrices.

entry.title: Discrete Hodge Decomposition
entry.content:
This post discusses the discrete-equivalent of Hodge decomposition from the paper "Topological Signal Processing Over Simplicial Complexes" by S. Barbarossa and S. Sardellitti.

This post continues introducing properties of graph Laplacians from the paper “Topological Signal Processing Over Simplicial Complexes” by S. Barbarossa and S. Sardellitti. Note that the presentation below is highly non-rigorous.

In the last post, we have introduced the definition of the graph Laplacian on signals over \(k\)-simplices via the incidence matrices:

$$ \boxed{ L_k = B_k^{\mathsf{T}} B_k + B_{k+1} B_{k+1}^{\mathsf{T}} }. $$

This equation seems a bit hard to remember, but it is in fact really simple if you remember what the domain and range of \(B _k\) is. Denote all the signals over \(k\)-simplices as \(\mathcal{S} _k\) (it is a vector space). Then,

$$ \begin{aligned} &B_k : \mathcal{S}_k \rightarrow \mathcal{S}_{k-1},\\ &L_k:\mathcal{S}_k \rightarrow \mathcal{S}_k. \end{aligned} $$

Homology

We can construct the following chain \(\mathcal{S}\):

$$ \cdots\rightarrow \mathcal{S}_{k+1} \xrightarrow{B_{k+1}} \mathcal{S}_{k} \xrightarrow{B_{k}} \mathcal{S}_{k-1} \rightarrow \cdots, $$

with

$$ \mathrm{im}(B_{k+1}) \subseteq \mathrm{ker}(B_{k}) $$

since \(B _{k} B _{k+1} = 0\).

Hodge decomposition

This chain structure naturally admits a decomposition on any signal \(s^k \in \mathcal{S} _k\).

$$ \boxed{ \mathcal{S}_k \cong \mathrm{im}(B_k^\mathsf{T}) \oplus \mathrm{ker}(L_k) \oplus \mathrm{im}(B_{k+1}) }. $$

The derivation is as below: by utilizing the linear algebra identity

$$ \mathrm{ker}(A)^\perp = \mathrm{row}(A) = \mathrm{col}(A^\mathsf{T}) = \mathrm{im}(A^\mathsf{T}), $$

we have

$$ \begin{align*} \mathcal{S}_k &= \mathrm{ker}(B_k) \oplus \mathrm{ker}(B_k)^\perp \\ &= \mathrm{ker}(B_k) \oplus \mathrm{im}(B_k^\mathsf{T}) \\ &= \mathrm{im}(B_{k+1}) \oplus \left(\mathrm{im}(B_{k+1})^\perp \cap \mathrm{ker}(B_k)\right) \oplus \mathrm{im}(B_k^\mathsf{T}) \\ &\cong \mathrm{im}(B_{k+1}) \oplus \frac{\mathrm{ker}(B_k)}{\mathrm{im}(B_{k+1})} \oplus \mathrm{im}(B_k^\mathsf{T}) \\ &= \mathrm{im}(B_{k+1}) \oplus H^k(\mathcal{S}) \oplus \mathrm{im}(B_k^\mathsf{T}), \end{align*} $$

where \(H^k(\mathcal{S})\) is the \(k\)th homology of \(\mathcal{S}\). The isomorphism \(\cong\) is used as it implies the existence of a natural mapping between \(H^k(\mathcal{S})\) and \(\mathrm{im}(B _{k+1})^\perp \cap \mathrm{ker}(B _k)\).

Next, we need to connect the homology with the kernel of the Laplacian and show that they are isomorphic. Specifically, we want to establish

$$ \mathrm{ker}(B_k) = \mathrm{im}(B_{k+1}) \oplus \mathrm{ker}(L_k). $$

This can be obtained by noticing that \(\mathrm{im}(B _{k+1}) \perp \mathrm{im}(B _{k}^\mathsf{T})\). Henceforth, there is NO such \(s^k\) that satisfies all \(B _k s^k \neq 0\), \(B _{k+1}^\mathsf{T} s^k \neq 0\), and \(L_k s^k = 0\).

The dimension of \(\mathrm{ker}(L _k)\) is known as the Betti number of order \(k\).

Hodge decomposition of signals

For any signal \(s^k \in \mathcal{S}_k\), we can thus establish the following unique orthogonal decomposition of

$$ \boxed{ s^k = B_{k}^{\mathsf{T}} s^{k-1} + s_{\mathrm{H}}^{k} + B_{k+1} s^{k+1}, } $$

where \(s^{k-1} \in \mathcal{S} _{k-1}\), \(s^{k} _{\mathrm{H}} \in \mathcal{S} _{k}\), \(s^{k+1} \in \mathcal{S} _{k+1}\), and \(L _k s^k _{\mathrm{H}} = 0\).

The term \(s^k _\mathrm{H}\) satisfies the Laplace equation \(L _k s^k _{\mathrm{H}} = 0\). Henceforth, we term it the harmonic component.

Example

The case in which we are most familiar with is \(k=1\). It has a lot of analogies with what we have learned in vector calculus. The space \(\mathcal{S} _1\) represents all the edge signals over a graph.

For edge signals over a graph, we can define the \(\mathrm{curl}\) and \(\mathrm{div}\) on them. They are defined respectively as

$$ \boxed{ \begin{align*} \mathrm{curl}(s^1) &= B_{2}^\mathsf{T} s^1, \\ \mathrm{div}(s^1) &= B_{1} s^1. \end{align*} } $$

Such definition matches our prior knowledge in vector calculus: the curl transforms a signal from edges to faces, while the divergence transforms a signal from edges to vertices.

By \(B _1 B _2 = 0\), we have

$$ \begin{align*} \mathrm{ker}(\mathrm{curl}) &= \mathrm{im}(B_1^{\mathsf{T}}), \\ \mathrm{ker}(\mathrm{div}) &= \mathrm{im}(B_2). \end{align*} $$

Therefore,

$$ \mathcal{S}_1 = \mathrm{ker}(\mathrm{curl}) \oplus \mathrm{ker}(L_1) \oplus \mathrm{ker}(\mathrm{div}), $$

it is an orthogonal decomposition into curl-less, harmonic, and divergence-less components:

$$ s^1 = B_1^\mathsf{T}s^0 + s^1_\mathrm{H} + B_2 s^2. $$

The term \(B _1^\mathsf{T}s^0\) is curl-less, it may be called an irrotational component, and can be viewed as the discrete gradient of \(s^0\). The harmonic component \(s^1 _\mathrm{H}\) is both curl-less and divergence-less. The term \(B _2s^2\) is divergence-less, it may be called a solenoidal component, and can be viewed as the curl of \(s^2\).

Relation to differential geometry

The original Hodge theorem in differential geometry works with the cochain of differential forms over \(\mathcal{M}\)

$$ \cdots \rightarrow \Omega^{k-1} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{d}_{k-1}} \Omega^k \xrightarrow{\mathrm{d}_{k}} \Omega^{k+1} \rightarrow \cdots $$

instead.

Given the differential operator \(\mathrm{d}\), we define its adjoint operator as \(\delta_{k+1} = \mathrm{d}_{k} ^ *\). The Laplacian is defined accordingly:

$$ \Delta_k = \mathrm{d}_{k-1}\delta_{k} + \delta_{k+1}\mathrm{d}_{k} = \mathrm{d}\delta + \delta\mathrm{d}. $$

The Hodge decomposition goes like

$$ \Omega^k \cong \mathrm{im}(\delta_{k+1}) \oplus \mathrm{ker}(\Delta_k) \oplus \mathrm{im}(\mathrm{d}_{k-1}). $$

The dimension of \(\mathrm{ker}(\Delta _k)\) is the \(k\)th Betti number of the manifold \(\mathcal{M}\). It is a topological invariance.
entry.summary: This post discusses the discrete-equivalent of Hodge decomposition.

entry.title: Robbins-Monro Step-Size Condition
entry.content:
This post discusses a result on the Robbins-Monro step-size condition which guarantees convergence under a noisy gradient descent.

In dealing with iterations of the form

$$ x_{t+1} = x_{t} - \mu \boldsymbol{g}_t, $$

the iteration might not converge given a fixed step-size \(\mu\) and a gradient with noise denoted by \(\boldsymbol{g} _t\).

The Robbins-Monro condition on an iteration-dependent scheme ensures the convergence of the iteration above. Let the step-size on iteration \(t\) be \(\mu_t\), then the condition reads

$$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \mu_t =\infty, \\ \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \mu_t^2 < \infty. \end{aligned} } $$

Result

For this post specifically, it is to proof Lemma F.5 in the book “Adaptation, Learning, and Optimization over Networks” by Ali H. Sayed. This lemma can be used to show the convergence of the noisy gradient descent recursion above under Robbins-Monro condition by a few change of variables. The lemma is stated as:

Let \(x_t \ge 0\) denote a scalar deterministic sequence that satisfies the inequality recursion:

$$ x_{t+1} \le \left(1 - a_t\right) x_t + b_t\;\;\;\;\;(t\ge 0). $$

When the scalar sequences \(\{a _t, b _t\}\) satisfy the four conditions:

$$ \begin{aligned} &0 \le a_t < 1, &&b_t \ge 0, \\ &\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} a_t = \infty, &&\lim_{t\rightarrow\infty} \frac{b_t}{a_t} = 0, \end{aligned} $$

it holds that \(\lim _{t\rightarrow\infty} x _t = 0\).

When the scalar sequences \(\{a _t, b _t\}\) are of the form

$$ \begin{aligned} &a_t = \frac{c}{t}, &&b_t = \frac{d}{t^{p+1}} \end{aligned} $$

for \(c, d, p > 0\), it holds that for large \(t\), the sequence \(x _t\) converges to \(0\) at one of the following rates:

$$ \begin{cases} x_t \le \left(\frac{d}{c-p}\right) \frac{1}{t^p} + o\left(\frac{1}{t^p}\right), & c>p; \\ x_t = O\left(\frac{\log t}{t^p}\right), & c=p; \\ x_t = O\left(\frac{1}{t^c}\right), & c<p. \end{cases} $$

This result is an excerpt from “Introduction to Optimization” by B. Poljak. This post is to adapt the proof of the first part there to a more clear version. The second part is already pretty clearly written, so I’ll not include it.

Proof

Expand the recursion out:

$$ \begin{aligned} x_{t+1} &\le (1 - a_t) (1 - a_{t-1}) x_{t-1} + b_t + (1 - a_t) b_{t-1} \\ &\le \prod_{k=1}^{t} (1 - a_k) x_1 + \sum_{k=1}^{t} b_k \prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell). \end{aligned} $$

Note the abuse of notation where

$$ \prod_{\ell=t+1}^{t} (1-a_\ell) = 1. $$

Next, let us bound the two terms. For the first term:

$$ \begin{align*} 0 \le \prod_{k=1}^{t} (1 - a_k) &= \exp\left(\sum_{k=1}^{t} \log (1 - a_k)\right) \\ &\le \exp\left( - \sum_{k=1}^{t} a_k\right) \\ &\xrightarrow{t\rightarrow\infty} \exp(-\infty) = 0. \end{align*} $$

The second term is: let \(\beta _k = b _k / a _k\), with \(\beta _k\rightarrow 0\). Then,

$$ \begin{align*} \sum_{k=1}^{t} b_k \prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell) &= \sum_{k=1}^{t} \beta_k a_k \prod_{\ell = k+1}^{t} (1-a_\ell) \\ &= \sum_{k=1}^{t} \beta_k (1-(1-a_k)) \prod_{\ell = k+1}^{t} (1-a_\ell) \\ &= \sum_{k=1}^{t} \beta_k \left(\prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1-a_\ell) - \prod_{\ell =k}^{t} (1-a_\ell)\right). \end{align*} $$

For all \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists some \(N>0\) such that \(\beta_k < \varepsilon\) for all \(k > N\). Then,

$$ \begin{align*} 0 &\le \sum_{k=1}^{t} b_k \prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell) \\ &= \sum_{k=1}^{N} b_k \prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell) + \sum_{k=N+1}^{t} b_k \prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell) \\ &= \sum_{k=1}^{N} b_k \prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell) + \sum_{k=N+1}^{t} \beta_k \left(\prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell) - \prod_{\ell=k}^{t} (1 - a_\ell)\right) \\ &\le \max_{1\le k\le N}b_k \cdot \prod_{\ell = N+1}^{t} (1-a_\ell) + \max_{N < k\le t} \beta_k \sum_{k=N+1}^{t} \left(\prod_{\ell=k+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell) - \prod_{\ell=k}^{t} (1 - a_\ell)\right) \\ &= \max_{1\le k\le N}b_k \cdot \prod_{\ell = N+1}^{t} (1-a_\ell) + \max_{N < k\le t} \beta_k \cdot \left(1 - \prod_{\ell=N+1}^{t} (1 - a_\ell)\right) \\ &\xrightarrow{t\rightarrow\infty} 0 + \varepsilon. \end{align*} $$

Hence, we have successfully bounded the series.
entry.summary: This post discusses a result on the Robbins-Monro step-size condition which guarantees convergence under a noisy gradient descent.

entry.title: On Optimization Methods in Machine Learning

link.title: On Optimization Methods in Machine Learning

entry.content:
This post discusses some thoughts on common optimization methods used in machine learning, which includes SGD, momentum, RMSprop, and Adam.

This post discusses some thoughts I have after watching the some videos on the common optimization methods used in machine learning, including SGD, momentum, RMSprop, and Adam.

Since we are in the realm of machine learning, let us denote the variable to be \(W_t\), representing the weights at iteration \(t\). The step-size will be denoted by \(\eta\) and be termed as the learning rate. The gradient oracle is represented as \(g(\cdot)\).

Different optimization methods have their own advantage and disadvantage. Knowing the respective properties allows one to use the “right” method for specific task at hand.

Stochastic gradient descent

The most basic method of them all is the stochastic gradient descent. The usual gradient descent is calculated by having

$$ g(W) = \nabla J(W), $$

where \(J\) is the loss function. However, in the case of a learning problem, the exact value to \(J\) actually depends on the data distribution that we want to learn. Consider data being denoted by a random variable \(\boldsymbol{\xi}\), we have

$$ J(W) = \mathbb{E}_{\boldsymbol{\xi}}[Q(W;\boldsymbol{\xi})]. $$

The expectation can be approximated by an averaging over the whole dataset.

This might be pretty costly as there can be a lot of data. And calculating the average of so many terms only attributes to a single gradient step. Pretty inefficient, isn’t it. Instead, we can take the approximation by an average over a smaller batch. Or in the extreme case, take

$$ g(W_t) = \nabla_W Q(W_t;\boldsymbol{\xi}_t), \text{ and } W_{t+1} = W_{t} - \eta g(W_t). $$

This is stochastic gradient descent, or SGD for short. The stochasticity (randomness) originates from the gradient being computed using random sampling of the data distribution.

Disadvantage of SGD

Stuck at local minima?

One might assume that similar to the usual gradient descent, SGD also suffers from easily getting stuck in local minima. However, this is simply not the whole picture.

As depicted in this video by Welch Labs, for each different data sample \(\boldsymbol{\xi}_t\), the optimization loss landscape is different for each SGD update iteration. In order for SGD to be stuck at a local minima, it is required that it be stuck at the same point for ALL samples \(\boldsymbol{\xi}\)’s. Which, if you think about it, is not an easy task.

The stochasticity inherent within the method actually helps kick the iteration away from local minima.

Slow convergence

An important disadvantage to SGD that is mentioned by both videos is its slow convergence to the global / local minima.

As \(W_{t}\) gets closer and closer to the global / local minima, the size of the gradient becomes smaller and smaller and the movement becomes slower and slower. We need a way to improve this, to make the descent go a bit faster.

Momentum

The momentum methods addresses the slow convergence of SGD near minima by including intertia into the update law. The momentum \(M_t\) represents the velocity of a point, viz., the update law is \(W_{t+1} = W_{t} - \eta M_{t}\).

The movement \(M_t\) at a point \(W_t\) is not only determined by its current gradient \(g(W_t)\), but also by the movement / momentum from previous iteration \(M_{t-1}\). The update law for GD with momentum is thus

$$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} M_{t} &= \beta M_{t-1} + (1-\beta) g(W_t), \\ W_{t+1} &= W_{t} - \eta M_{t}. \end{aligned} } $$

The size of the inertia is denoted by \(\beta\), often given the value of \(0.9\). The new momentum is a weighted sum of previous momentum and current gradient. The inclusion of such a weighted sum allows for faster convergence speed when one has gradients pointing in similar directions across iterations.

The disadvantage of momentum will be discussed later when we are considering Adam.

Nesterov momentum

The Nesterov momentum can be viewed as an optimistic momentum descent. The mathematical formulation is as below:

$$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} M_{t} &= \beta M_{t-1} + (1-\beta) g(W_t - \eta M_{t-1}), \\ W_{t+1} &= W_{t} - \eta M_{t}. \end{aligned} } $$

The optimism here means that the Nesterov momentum looks ahead and computes the gradient at a point that the algorithm predicts it to be at instead. As computing the momentum at the predicted point actually gives a more accurate description of the future behavior, this look-ahead might give a finer adjustment to the momentum and improve the convergence rate (if the loss function is well-suited).

RMSprop

In optimizing a function of the form

$$ J(x_1,x_2) = 100 x_1^2 + 0.01 x_2^2, $$

the gradient computed in the \(x_1\) direction will be way larger than the gradient in the \(x_2\) direction. This seems unfair! We should make the update in both direction be of same size as they are both just minimizing a quadratic function. How can we normalize the gradients? The root-mean-square propagation method considers exactly this.

Consider the \(i\)th parameter \([W _t] _i\), its update law is

$$ \boxed{ \begin{align*} [ v _{t+1}] _{i} &= \beta [v_{t}]_i + (1-\beta) [g(W_t)]_i^2, \\ [W_{t+1}] _{i} &= [W_{t}]_i - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{[v_{t+1}]_i} + \varepsilon} \cdot [g(W_{t})]_i. \end{align*} } $$

The vector \(v_t\) records the (root-mean-square) variance of each component of the update, it is updated by weighted average. The small number \(\varepsilon\) is for numerical stability. Note that components with a larger RMS is dampened to be smaller, while those that have smaller RMS is enhanced.

The disadvantage to RMSprop is a bit similar to that of SGD.

Note

The Muon optimizer with its Newton-Schulz iteration and Finsler manifold optimization actually has the same spirit has RMSprop: to normalize the update directions.

Adam

Adam is a combination of both momentum and RMSprop.

Further more, as stated in the paper “Old Optimizer, New Norm: An Anthology”, Adam is simply the combination of (1) an optimization over the \(\ell_\infty\)-norm, which corresponds to the normalization of RMSprop, and (2) the exponential moving average (EMA), which corresponds to the weighted sum of present and past mean and variance of step in momentum methods.

Disadvantage of momentum

Besides going too fast so that the iteration might escape of minima (which can also be a benefit in some scenarios), momentum methods suffer from cases where averaging is not beneficial.

In applications such as reinforcement learning, the environment to the system might change abruptly. In these cases, the past momentum information bear little to none resemblance to the present gradient. The weighted sum between previous momentum and present gradient might lead the update astray from the right direction, thus worsening the performance.

In conclusion, momentum allows faster speed to convergence when close to the minima and the optimization landscape is not changing, while RMSprop allows for normalized sizes of update across all coordinates.
entry.summary: This post discusses some thoughts on common optimization methods used in machine learning, which includes SGD, momentum, RMSprop, and Adam.

entry.title: Graph Laplacian
entry.content:
This is a series of posts on connecting techniques of differential geometry and signal processing over graphs. This post specifically addresses the notion of differential operator over graphs and graph Laplacians. For more details, one can refer to the paper “Topological Signal Processing Over Simplicial Complexes” by S. Barbarossa and S. Sardellitti.

In the previous post, we addressed the basic notations to simplices over graphs. This second post of the series

Incidence matrix

Next, we consider signals on the simplices. A signal on \(k\)-simplices is denoted by \(s^k\). Assume there are \(n\) \(k\)-simplices, then \(s^k \in \mathbb{C}^{n\times1}\).

Next, let us introduce the incidence matrix \(B_k\) operating on signals on \(k\)-simplices, mapping them to signals on \((k-1)\)-simplices. The incidence matrix operates as the boundary operation, mapping \(k\)-chains to \((k-1)\)-chains.

Consider the one-hot vector \(\boldsymbol{1} _{\sigma^k _i}\) representing the \(i\)th \(k\)-simplex, we want

$$ B_k \boldsymbol{1}_{\sigma^k_i} = \boldsymbol{1}_{\partial_k\sigma^k_i}. $$

Formally, the entries to \(B_k\) are defined as

$$ \boxed{ [B_k]_{ij} = \begin{cases} +1, & \sigma^{k-1}_i \subset \sigma^k_j \text{ and } \sigma^{k-1}_i \sim \sigma^k_j \\ -1, & \sigma^{k-1}_i \subset \sigma^k_j \text{ and } \sigma^{k-1}_i \not\sim \sigma^k_j \\ 0, & \sigma^{k-1}_i \not\subset \sigma^k_j. \end{cases} } $$

From the property of the boundary operation, we have that

$$ B_{k-1} B_{k} = 0. $$

The incidence matrix represents the adjacency information between the simplices.

Graph Laplacian

The combinatorial Laplacian matrix acting on signals over \(k\)-simplices is defined as

$$ \boxed{ L_k = B_k^{\mathsf{T}} B_k + B_{k+1} B_{k+1}^{\mathsf{T}} }. $$

The first component \(B _k^{\mathsf{T}} B _k\) is the lower Laplacian, representing the lower adjacency; while the latter component \(B _{k+1} B _{k+1}^{\mathsf{T}}\) is the upper Laplacian, representing the upper adjacency.

I will postpone the discussion as to why it is defined so until I can present an easy to understand explanation. But for the mean time, I am simply surprised by the elegance and simplicity of the representation, as it greatly mirrors its differential geometrical counterpart – the Laplace–Beltrami operator.

With the Laplacian, one can perform Fourier transform on the signals by a coordinate transform with respect to the eigenbasis of the Laplacian.

Moreover, the Hodge decomposition of differential forms may also be introduced. For a thorough discussion, however, some knowledge on differential geometry is required. So I guess I will postpone the related discussion to the far future.

I decided to learn about topological signal processing as I was preparing for the talk on Fourier transform. This is such an interesting topic, but I have to find some applications to it so as to push myself in learning it.
entry.summary: This is a series of posts on connecting techniques of differential geometry and signal processing over graphs. This post specifically addresses the notion of differential operator over graphs and graph Laplacians.
